title: practical english usage - Part 330
content:
Last week, month, etc (without the) is the week, month, etc just before this one.

1 last     week,      month,       etc;   the    last     week,      month,       etc 505 last,    the    last,    the    latest ► large,     great      and   big     io  > ►           1    3.

For ways of using you know,     30 (= ɪ  know the restaurant.) 7 went to a nice restaurant called The Elizabeth last night! * I know it!

I know it generally refers to things - it replaces a noun.

’ You're late! ’ I know! (= I know that I’ m  late.) I know refers to facts - it could be completed by a ftet-clause.

Note the difference between these two short answers.

6 / know       and   I know       it from his aavni.) not He's from Liverpool, as you can tell from his accent, (     ... as you can -kite not countries, (     .. .-and know people-. ..) I want to travel round the world and get to know people from different know that?) not ’ She's married! ’ Where did you find that out? (     .. .-Where-did-you we  can use for example find out, get to know, learn, hear, can tell.

something is to have learnt it, not to learn it. To  talk about getting knowledge Know is not normally used to talk about finding something out: to know 5 know      and   find     out,    etc not We all know about Abraham Lincoln, (     We all knew Abraham Lincoln!} ’ You don't know my mother, do you? ’ No, I've never met her!

have heard of or another structure. Compare: direct personal experience. In other cases, we  normally use know ɑbout/of Know + object is used mainly to talk about knowledge that comes from 4 know       and   know       about/of Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  506  later and in Bye! See you later!

But without a time expression, later can be used to mean ‘ after now’ .

I'll see you in a few days, (     I'll see you a few-days later:) Penny's coming on July 1st, and Tom will arrive about a week later.

She got married on her 18th birthday; six months later she was divorced.

to mean ‘ after now’ . Compare: With a time expression, we generally use later to mean ‘ after that time’ , and in 506 later      and   in For tenses with This is the last lime. .., etc, ► But it doesn't pay as much  as his last one.

- He's enjoying his latest job. (     He's enjoying his last job:) She thinks it's much  better than her last one.

- Her latest book's being published next week, (     Her Jost-book-...) before’ . Compare: We   can use latest to talk about something new, and last to mean ‘ the one 4 latest      and   last This is going to be the last Christmas I'll spend at home.

In the last week of the trip something funny happened.

The last can also mean 'the last in a series’ .

3 the   last   in  a  series not The last few days have been busy, (     The last days. ..) We generally say the last few days! weeks, etc, not the last days! weeks, etc.

I've been busy for the last three months, (     .. .for-the three last monti Note the word order in expressions with numbers.

2  the    last     three      . . ., etc PAST   t  Mon        Tu     Wed     Thurs           Fri   Sat   Sun       Mon        Tu     Wed     Thurs           1  NOW the last week ►'  PAST   ɪ   Mon        Tu     Wed     Thurs           Fri   Sat   Sun       Mon        Tu     Wed     Thurs          "  NOW^ last week The  difference between next and the next is similar. ►    533 We'ue lived here for the last year, and we're very happy with the place.

- We bought this house last year.

I've had a cold for the last week. 1 feel terrible.

- I was ill last week, but I'm OK  this week, (     I was ill the last week...) Compare: vocabulary  •  508  learn It's time you learnt (how) to change the oil in the car.

She enjoyed learning (how) to look after young animals.

we  can use learn to . .. or learn how to...

To  talk about consciously learning a method or technique for doing something, 2 learn      (how)    to...

For the adjective learned (/'ɪɜ:nid/), ►     191.

American English (learn/learned). For other verbs like this, ►     1.2.
